coredata.dc:title: Manufacture of nano graphite oxides derived from aqueous glucose solutions and in-situ synthesis of magnetite–graphite oxide composites
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiang
Zhao, Tiantian
Liu, Pengpeng
Cui, Ping
Hu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: A “bottom up” approach of manufacturing graphite oxides (GOs) derived from aqueous glucose solutions by virtue of an environmentally-friendly process and the way of in-situ synthesizing magnetite–GOs composites are described in this work in detail. The dehydrations among glucose molecules under hydrothermal condition result in the initial carbon quantum dots and ultimate GOs. The structural information of the GOs is obtained by the infrared, ultraviolet–visible and X-ray photoelectron spectra. The magnetite–GOs composites were obtained by a one-pot method under the same hydrothermal conditions as the one of preparing GOs. The composites perform high activities in catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B in the presence of hydrogen peroxides without extra heating or pH adjusting. Both the GOs and the magnetite–GOs composites are also assured by measurements of transmission electron microscope and X-ray powder diffraction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite materials
Chemical synthesis
Chemical techniques
Surface properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials and preparation of GOs and magnetite–GOs composites
Degradation of RhB with the catalyst of magnetite–GOs dispersions
Preparation of GOs derived from aqueous glucose solutions
Generation of the magnetite–GOs composites with one-pot method
Degradation of RhB solutions catalyzed by the magnetite–GOs dispersions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Training Programs of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates
Educational Commission of Anhui Province of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Educational Commission of Anhui Province of China ( KJ2012A051 ) and National Training Programs of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of the GOs obtained by reaction time of 3 and 4 h corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively. The heights of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and the initial GOs sheets in (a) are displayed respectively in (c) and (d). The height of the GOs sheets in (b) is shown in (e).
TEM image and XRD pattern of the GOs obtained by hydrothermal reaction of 4 h corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively.
The GOs and magnetite–GOs composites are demonstrated by IR spectra of (a) and (b) and by UV–vis spectra of (c) and (d) respectively.

Schematic descriptions about the formation of the magnetite–GOs composites. The produced CQDs integrate with Fe(OH)2 and Fe(OH)3 via the intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Large amounts of such integrations are dehydrated under hydrothermal condition leading to generations of the nano magnetites and GOs. The insetted TEM image demonstrates the magnetite nanoparticles are coated by the GOs sheets.
TEM image, TGA curve and XRD pattern of the magnetite–GOs composites corresponding to (a), (b) and (c) respectively. Magnetism of the composites is demonstrated in the inset of (a).
XPS survey of the magnetite–GOs composites is shown in (a). The high-resolution scans of C 1s, O 1s and Fe 2p are shown in (b), (c) and inset of (a) respectively.

Successive UV–vis spectra of (a) correspond with the degradation of RhB solutions catalyzed by the magnetite–GOs dispersions. The absorbances of the decomposing system depending on degradation time are displayed in (b) in the presence of mixture of the magnetite and the GOs, the sole magnetite and the sole GOs.

The GOs were obtained by hydrothermal reaction of aqueous glucoses solutions at 180 °C. The magnetite–GOs composites were synthesized with the mixtures of glucoses, Fe(III) and Fe(II) under the same conditions. These composites performed well in catalytic degradations of aqueous RhB solutions in the presence of hydrogen peroxides at room temperature.

head.ce:title: Manufacture of nano graphite oxides derived from aqueous glucose solutions and in-situ synthesis of magnetite–graphite oxide composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A “bottom up” approach of manufacturing graphite oxides (GOs) derived from aqueous glucose solutions by virtue of an environmentally-friendly process and the way of in-situ synthesizing magnetite–GOs composites are described in this work in detail. The dehydrations among glucose molecules under hydrothermal condition result in the initial carbon quantum dots and ultimate GOs. The structural information of the GOs is obtained by the infrared, ultraviolet–visible and X-ray photoelectron spectra. The magnetite–GOs composites were obtained by a one-pot method under the same hydrothermal conditions as the one of preparing GOs. The composites perform high activities in catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B in the presence of hydrogen peroxides without extra heating or pH adjusting. Both the GOs and the magnetite–GOs composites are also assured by measurements of transmission electron microscope and X-ray powder diffraction.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphite oxides (GOs) are extremely interesting materials which attract considerable researchers to study their preparation techniques, chemical structures and potential applications [1]. The unique properties of GOs are arisen from their unique structure, such as the quasi-2D lattice with many conjugated units and the plentiful functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups, on their basal planes or edges. The conventional methods of preparing GOs are comprised by the pyrolysis or exfoliation of graphite powders with strong oxidants of concentrated sulfuric acid and potassium permanganate [2,3], called “top down” method. However, the pyrolysis of graphite brought few functional groups on the surface of GOs which were unhelpful for their dispersions in aqueous media. The Hummers technique [4] were used widely in obtaining water-dispersible GOs since the vast oxygen-contained functional groups were produced when graphite powders were oxidized by strong oxidants under a high temperature. However, the exfoliation of graphite with strong oxidants will bring some poisonous materials into environment inevitably. In recent years, some novel approaches were invented for manufacturing GOs. Il-Kwon Oh and his coauthors reported an eco-friendly method of preparing graphene sheets by volume expansion under microwave irradiation on graphite powders [5]. The intensive oxidations were avoided and the GOs nanosheets with big sizes could be produced. Chemical vapor deposition of a sodium ethoxide solution [6] or methane [7] was another approach of manufacturing graphene nanosheets. Graphene nanoribbons could be grown on gold surface via thermally induced polymerization [8]. 10,10′-Dibromo-9,9′-bianthracene could be butted to form a big one with a wider conjugated plane catalyzed by the Au catalyst. Citric acids could also be utilized in preparing GOs when it was heated at 200 °C for several hours [9]. The high temperature caused intermolecular dehydrations since citric acids are rich in carboxyl groups. This suggests that cheap hydrocarbon compounds with hydroxyl or carboxyl groups may be qualified candidates for preparing GOs. Such “bottom up” methods [10,11] enriched the approaches of preparing GOs which avoid the use of poisonous materials. Herein, we will report convenient ways of preparing GOs by hydrothermal reaction of aqueous solutions of glucoses and manufacturing the magnetite–GOs composites with a one-pot method. Such magnetite–GOs composites are applied in the catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB), which used as a model pollutant. The whole process is described schematically in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and preparation of GOs and magnetite–GOs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Anhydrous glucose (99%), ferric chlorides hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O, 99%) and ammonia (NH3·H2O, 26–28%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai). All chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

About 0.5 g anhydrous glucose were dissolved in 15 mL water. Hydrothermal reaction was carried out in an electronic drier at 180 °C for 3 h or 4 h. The products were filtrated with a polytetrafluoroethylene membrane (0.22 μm) and washed with copious water for at least ten times to remove any excessive glucose and dried at 60 °C.

Similarly, 0.5 g glucose, 1 mL 0.5 M FeCl3 (0.5 mmol) and 50 mg FeCl2·4H2O (0.25 mmol) were mixed in 15 mL water with an ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Then the mixture was alkalified with ammonia (13%) until the pH reached 8.0. The reaction proceeded at 180 °C for 4 h to manufacture the aqueous dispersions of magnetite–GOs composites. Such dispersions were applied directly in the catalytic degradation of RhB. The magnetite–GOs composites were obtained by filtration and thorough washings with copious water. Ultimately, they were dried at 60 °C for characterizations. The nano magnetites were also prepared from the mixture of 1 mL 0.5 M FeCl3 and 50 mg FeCl2·4H2O in 15 mL water under the same hydrothermal conditions for detecting the catalysis of the sole magnetites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of RhB with the catalyst of magnetite–GOs dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: About 1 mL magnetite–GOs dispersion (pH = 7.0) was added to 20 mL aqueous RhB solutions (10 mg L−1) with a magnetic stirring. Then 1 mL H2O2 was put into the mixture without any pH adjusting. The degradation of RhB solutions were executed at room temperature (12 °C). The degenerating progresses were detected with successive measurements of UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra. The catalytic reactions of the sole GOs, the prepared nano magnetites as well as the mixture of the GOs and the nano magnetites were also tested with such degradations to understand the principle of the catalysis. Absorbance changes at 554 nm were recorded to investigate the catalytic effects.

The obtained GOs and magnetite–GOs composites were characterized by infrared (IR) (Nicolet 380, Thermo Nicolet Corporation) and UV–vis (specord 200, Analytic Jena AG) spectra. The progresses of forming GOs were also checked with atomic force microscopes (AFM) (Veeco Instruments Inc.) with tapping mode in air at room temperature and analyzed using the Nanoscope software. Such progresses were also demonstrated by photographing in a darkroom under irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) and emission spectra executed by fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS 45, PerkinElmer). The GOs and the composites were also estimated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (XPert Pro MPD, PANalytical B.V.). The magnetite–GOs composites were ascertained by measurement of transmission electron microscope (TEM) (H7650, Hitachi Company) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Thermax 700, Thermo Fisher Scientific) at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen environment. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) (KRATOS AMICUS) of the composites were recorded by K-Alpha with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source. Wide scan of 0–1000 eV and high-resolution detections on C 1s and O 1s were executed to obtain the elemental intergradient with nonlinear least-square fitting using different weighting of Gaussian–Lorentzian functions. The degradations of RhB catalyzed by the magnetite–GOs composites were judged by successive measurements of UV–vis spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GOs derived from aqueous glucose solutions
ce:section.ce:para: As we all known that the crystalline form of an aldose is normally a cyclic hemiacetal. In solution, the aldose exists as an equilibrium mixture of the cyclic hemiacetal with the open-chain form. The equilibrium favors the cyclic hemiacetal form. However, hydrothermal disposing with a high temperature may change the shift direction of the equilibrium. The resulted open-chain form benefits the approaches of the glucose molecules. The rich hydroxyl and aldehyde groups also favor the interactions among glucose molecules through the occurrence of H-bonding interactions. Thus the intermolecular dehydrations are apt to take place in violent circumstances. For example, the CQDs could be produced by the microwave irradiation of aqueous glucose solutions within tens of minutes [12]. Under such a hydrothermal condition, CQDs are generated very easily which judged by AFM image illuminated in Fig. 1(a) and their photoluminescences with an irradiation of a portable ultraviolet lamp. When reaction time reaches 3 h, large amounts of the CQDs are generated. The bright spots in Fig. 1(a) demonstrate the CQDs in the solutions. The sizes of the CQDs are 2–3 nm, exhibited in Fig. 1(c). Few GOs sheets are produced during this period, which can be seen in Fig. 1(a). And the height is about 2.5 nm, shown in Fig. 1(d). With the prolonged reaction time the amounts of the CQDs decline, which demonstrated in Fig. 1(b). It discloses that the most CQDs are integrated into the GOs sheets when the reaction time reaches 4 h. The height of the GOs sheet reaches 3 nm, shown in Fig. 1(e). The value is greater than the theoretical height of a single GOs sheet. This shows that the GOs sheets are tend to stack spontaneously which illuminates the planar structure of the GOs and strong interactions among the GOs sheets. Such a process can be also demonstrated by the color changes of the solution and the corresponding photoluminescent photographs shown in Fig. S1 of the Supporting information. It is clear that the color become deep gradually with prolonged reaction time. When reaction time reaches 4 h the floccules appear in the solution which forecasts the production of GOs sheets. The photoluminescent photographs of Fig. S1 show that reaction time of 1 h brings blue fluorescence while the 3 h produces green one. This suggests the growth of CQDs in size and is demonstrated by the occurred shoulder peak near 500 nm shown in Fig. S2(c). Fig. S2 demonstrate the photoluminescences of different stages of the reaction. The fluorescent spectrum shown in Fig. S2(d) has no obvious alterations compared with (a∼c). This illustrates the development of the CQDs in size reaches a summit and declares that not all the CQDs can be integrated into GOs, which agrees with the analyses of the AFM images of Fig. 1.

The GOs are also exhibited in TEM image of Fig. 2(a) . The sizes of the most GOs are less than 100 nm. Apparently, such GOs sheets are much smaller than the one obtained by exfoliation of graphite powders [3,5]. Our experiments demonstrated that the GOs have better dispersion stability in aqueous media than the GOs obtained by Hummers technique. This is attributed to the nano-sized GOs and plentiful hydrophilic functional groups of the GOs. It is difficult to produce micron-sized GOs with this “bottom up” method according to our experiences. XRD pattern of Fig. 2(b) shows a sharp peak at 2θ = 11.6° corresponding to the (001) reflection of the GOs which is also similar to the GOs obtained by chemical vapor deposition or Hummers technique [6,13–16].

IR spectrum of Fig. 3(a) discloses the structural information. The intensive peak centered at 3440 cm−1 and a peak at 1385 cm−1 should be arisen from the stretching and rocking vibration of O–H respectively. The hydroxyls should come from the produced GOs and the adsorbed vapors since the GOs are rich in hydrophilic functional groups. Band 1024 cm−1 is due to the vibration of C–O. A pronounced peak at 1636 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of CC species. This indicates that intramolecular dehydration occurs simultaneously during the GOs formation which is beneficial for generating conjugated structures. Absorption at 1693 cm−1 is due to the aldehyde group which comes from glucose molecules. The aldehyde groups of GOs are demonstrated by a silver mirror reaction. During the reaction, we can not observe the silver mirror on the wall of the test tube. But the color of the mixture of tollens reagent and the GOs changes from brown to dark when heating. Since the obtained GOs nanosheets have an excellent solubility in water shown in the inset of Fig. 2(a), the resulted silver particles are dispersed very well and can not deposit on the wall of the test tube. That is the reason why we can not observe the silver mirror.

The UV–vis of Fig. 3(c) also supports aforementioned analyses. The intensive peak of 282 nm and the shoulder peak of 220 nm are assigned respectively to n–π* and π–π* transition [17,18]. This declaims the existence of the conjugated system which agrees with the IR adsorption of CC. Such UV–vis characters are similar to the GOs obtained by Hummers technique [18], which suggest that the conjugated structures are alike in both GOs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of the magnetite–GOs composites with one-pot method
ce:section.ce:para: One-pot method of preparing magnetite–GOs hybrids is executed according to the co-precipitation way under the same hydrothermal condition as the one of preparing the GOs. The schematic description is shown in Fig. 4 . Firstly, Fe(III) and Fe(II) are precipitated simultaneously giving Fe(OH)3 and Fe(OH)2 respectively when the pH of the solution reaches 8.0. In hydrothermal condition, CQDs are manufactured soon. A carbon quantum dot integrate with several Fe(OH)3 and Fe(OH)2 by H-bonding between the oxygen-contained groups of the CQDs and the –OH of the precipitates. Dehydration reactions take place among the CQDs under such a circumstance giving GOs, accompanied by the reaction of 2Fe(OH)3 + Fe(OH)2 → Fe3O4 + 4H2O. The inserted TEM image of Fig. 4 shows clearly that the resulted magnetites are coated completely by the GOs sheets. The GOs substrates hold back the aggregation of the produced magnetites. This plays an important role in catalytic reactions.

The obtained hybrids are demonstrated by the IR spectrum of Fig. 3(b), UV–vis spectrum of Fig. 3(d), TEM of Fig. 5(a) and XRD pattern of Fig. 5(c). The peak at 607 cm−1 of Fig. 3(b) should be due to the stretching vibration of Fe–O. The wide absorption, ranged from ultraviolet to visible region shown in Fig. 3(d), should be attributed to the character of magnetite nanoparticles [19]. Bands of 220 and 282 nm arisen from the GOs remain intact. TEM image of Fig. 5(a) shows that the nano magnetites are coated entirely by the GOs. The inset photograph of Fig. 5(a) proves the magnetism of the magnetite–GO composites. TGA curve of Fig. 5(b) shows that about 3% mass loss when the temperature reaches 200 °C. It is quite different from the GOs obtained by Hummers technique which have a more large mass loss when heated at 200 °C [13,20]. This discloses that water molecules are apt to be enclosed in large-sized GOs sheets. When the temperature is among 200–550 °C the mass loss increases steadily which means the removal of oxygenic functional groups. When the temperature is higher than 550 °C the GOs are decomposed rapidly. The mass loss becomes slow when the temperature is higher than 720 °C, which suggests the GOs are decomposed completely and a high percentage of magnetite in the nano composites. X-ray diffraction pattern of Fig. 5(c) reveals the diffraction peaks at 30.0, 35.4, 37.2, 43.0, 53.4, 57.0 and 62.5°. These contribute to crystal planes of (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511) and (440) [21,22] respectively and are well consistent with standard X-ray data for the magnetite phase (JCPDS no. 19-0629).

The components of the magnetite–GOs nanocomplexes are also ascertained by XPS analyses. XPS survey of Fig. 6(a) exhibits C 1s, O 1s and Fe 2p components clearly. The binding energy of 711.2 and 724.4 eV are corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 respectively. These values are very close with the ones of Fe 2p of magnetite on GOs described in the literature [23]. The C 1s core-level spectrum of Fig. 5(b) can be curve-fitted with three components having binding energies at 284.7, 285.8 and 288.5 eV, attributable to C–C–C, C–C–O (derived from C–O–C or C–OH) and C O (derived from aldehyde group) respectively [24–26]. This also demonstrates the aldehyde groups of the GOs. The high-resolution scan of O 1s also gives three components by the curve-fittings with peaks at 530.2, 532.0 and 533.7 eV which assigned to O of GOs [14], FeO and Fe2 O 3 respectively. Interestedly, the Fe 2p signal can not be detected without sample grinding during the measurements. This illuminates that the magnetite are covered completely by the GOs, which is consistent with the analyses of TEM images of Figs. 4 and 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of RhB solutions catalyzed by the magnetite–GOs dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: The RhB solutions are decomposed rapidly within half an hour which shown in Fig. 7(a) . This tells us that the magnetite–GOs solutions perform catalysis wonderfully in degradation of RhB solutions. Plentiful functional groups and the planar structure of the GOs provide excellent platforms for loading the synthesized magnetites. Hence the resulted nano magnetites are dispersed uniformly on surface of GOs. According to our experimental results such 1 mL magnetite–GOs dispersion can be used at least for eight times in catalyzing the degradation of RhB. In addition, the solutions of the sole GOs or magnetites do not display the catalysis on decomposing of RhB, which proved by Fig. 7(b). The direct mixtures of the GOs and the magnetites can not bring an obvious acceleration of the degradation which also exhibited in Fig. 7(b). These confirm that the sole component of nano magnetites or GOs can not catalyze the degradation. The one-pot method brings an obvious enhancement of the catalytic activation on account of the real contact of the two components and the homogeneous dispersion of the magnetic nanoparticles. These facilitate the decomposing reaction. Due to the huge specific surface areas of the GOs RhB molecules are apt to be absorbed via π–π stacking effect or polar interaction between the GOs and RhB. During the degradation, hydrogen peroxides were absorbed firstly by such in-situ synthesized nanoparticles and catalytically decomposed into radicals of HO· on surfaces of the magnetite. Therefore the absorbed RhB can be decomposed quickly by such magnetite-decorated GOs without pH adjusting [27]. According to these analyses, the GOs and the loaded nano magnetites play different roles in degradation of RhB. It is worth mentioning that mixture of the nano magnetites and the GOs did not exhibit the catalytic effect which also demonstrated by Fig. 7(b). This suggests that the in-situ synthesis benefits for the production of the nano magnetite–GOs hybrids with high catalytic activities.

Nano GOs can be prepared via a facile approach of hydrothermal disposing of aqueous glucose solutions. Plentiful aldehyde and hydroxyl groups will widen the applications of the GOs through proper chemical modifications. The well dispersion in water facilitates the preparation of magnetite–GOs nano hybrids by the way of one-pot method. The hybrids perform a wonderful catalysis on degradation of RhB within half an hour without extra heating or pH adjusting. The direct mixing of the GOs and the magnetites can not bring the decomposition of aqueous RhB, which demonstrates that the in-situ synthesized nano hybrids possess catalysis of producing radicals of HO· in the presence of hydrogen peroxides. In Fenton reaction [27], excessive iron ion should be removed after accomplishment of the treatment otherwise they will be a new pollution source. This requires additional separation steps and increase of the treatment costs partly because they are completely resolved in water. The magnetite–GOs nano hybrids, as heterogeneous catalysts, may overcome this drawback and become economical and highly efficient catalysts in treating organic pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Educational Commission of Anhui Province of China (KJ2012A051) and National Training Programs of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2015.01.004.
